candidate_sources.title: Ascochyta Leaf Blight | CSU Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Ascochyta leaf blight results in the rapid development of large irregularly shaped, straw-colored patches on Kentucky bluegrass and occasionally on tall fescue and perennial ryegrass during the summer. Leaves often exhibit a bleached tip dieback that extends about a third to halfway down the leaf blade.

candidate_sources.title: Ascochyta Leaf Blight of Turf
candidate_sources.snippet: Ascochyta blight is a sporadic disease that can infect Kentucky bluegrass, tall fescue and perennial ryegrass. The disease is caused by nearly 80 different species of Ascochyta. Blighting is usually restricted to the leaves; bluegrass crowns and roots typically are not killed.

candidate_sources.title: Ascochyta leaf blight | MU Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Ascochyta spp. produce minute yellow to dark brown, flask-shaped fruiting bodies (pycnidia) in diseased leaf tissue. Symptoms may develop throughout the growing season but are more common during hot, droughty periods that were preceded by cool, rainy conditions. Because Ascochyta spp. are primarily foliar pathogens, diseased turfgrass usually recovers relatively quickly.

candidate_sources.title: Ascochyta Leaf Blight â€“ Uncommon & Sometimes Devastating - Turf Pathology | NC State Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Ascochyta Leaf Blight on Kentucky bluegrass â€” uncommon but sometimes devastating. Discusses symptoms, identification with pycnidia, and management strategies for cool-season turfgrass.

candidate_sources.title: Ascochyta (leaf blight of turfgrass) - Bugwoodwiki
candidate_sources.snippet: Ascochyta spp. survive as conidia in pycnidia on dead leaves or clippings remaining in the thatch; thousands of conidia ooze from a single pycnidium during wet weather and are dispersed by splashing rain, irrigation, mowing or other management activities.

candidate_sources.title: Draft Genome Sequences of 11 Isolates of Ophiosphaerella spp., the Causal Agents of Several Turfgrass Diseases Including Spring Dead Spot of Bermudagrass and Necrotic Ring Spot of Kentucky Bluegrass
candidate_sources.snippet: Ophiosphaerella herpotricha, O. korrae, and O. narmari are the causal agents of spring dead spot of warm-season grasses worldwide, and O. korrae is the causal agent of necrotic ring spot of cool-season grasses (including Kentucky bluegrass) that primarily occurs in North and South America.

candidate_sources.title: Necrotic Ring Spot of Kentucky Bluegrass | Colorado State University Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Necrotic ring spot (NRS) is a perennial fungal disease of Kentucky bluegrass caused by the fungal pathogen Ophiosphaerella korrae. NRS results in circular or doughnut-shaped patches of dead grass. Light green to straw-colored spots or patches several inches to several feet in diameter develop in the lawn. NRS can be suppressed by a combination of good turf management practices and fungicide applications.

candidate_sources.title: Ophiosphaerella spp. (spring dead spot on bermudagrass) - Bugwoodwiki
candidate_sources.snippet: Ophiosphaerella herpotricha, O. korrae, and O. narmari are the causal agents of spring dead spot on warm-season grasses; O. korrae also causes necrotic ring spot on cool-season grasses including Kentucky bluegrass.

candidate_sources.title: Necrotic Ring Spot of Poa Species | UMass Center for Agriculture, Food, and the Environment
candidate_sources.snippet: The fungus Ophiosphaerella korrae causes necrotic ring spot disease of bluegrasses (Poa species) and creeping red fescue. Initial symptoms are small (2-4 in), light green spots which enlarge into patches 1 to 3 feet in diameter; turf turns reddish brown to bronze and fades to straw color, often producing a frog-eye appearance as plants recolonize the patch center.

candidate_sources.title: Necrotic Ring Spot | Utah State University Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Necrotic ring spot (NRS), caused by Ophiosphaerella korrae, is one of the most destructive diseases of Kentucky bluegrass. The fungus infects roots and crowns; symptoms include circular patches of dead, straw-colored grass, often with surviving green tufts in the center (frog-eye pattern). Active in cool, wet spring and fall conditions.
